text:
I spent a week with Farza Majeed. In his own words, he's "just a guy that loves creating things for others", such as:

- a $100k/year eBay business selling CDs as a teenager
- Zipschool, a better way to homeschool your kids via live classes for 150,000 students
- Buildspace, a school for people to work on their own ideas, helping over 200,000 people become app developers, content creators, musicians, writers, or doing anything else they loved

Farza is like the internet's big brother. He makes it seem so simple to follow your passion, because he's because he's been doing it for years. He never gives advice, just tells his story and hopes people learn something from it. You look at him and think, "if he can do it, why can't I?"

Everyone in Silicon Valley wants something from you, but Farza literally hasn't charged his users for the past 5 years. I'm often shocked at how he's able to afford rent, yet I'm also fairly certain he's going to be a billionaire.

After burning out, he's back to founding another company. I wrote for @sfalexandria_ about his journey, how he changes the lives of everyone he meets, and what keeps him going.

https://t.co/WTsSn8G4ha

text:
workflow I'm having success with:

1) start in plan mode with Opus and make any obvious improvements

2) turn plan into .md file to share with Codex agent

3) have Codex review plan and give a confidence score out of 100 that it will work

4) share feedback with Opus and have it improve

5) run this loop until confidence score is ~90+

6) execute plan

text:
I’m not sure where the bottleneck for you is but here’s my setup and process. Hope it will help!
- I use Cursor and used specifically Opus 4.6 (for planning and big feature implementation) and Composer 2 for small tasks/edits
- Whenever, I start implementing a large new feature, I will always switch to planning mode and specifically ask it to create a plan that is robust and scalable.
- After Opus created a first draft of the plan, I will usually ask it to review the plan with fresh eyes to make sure that the plan is robust and scalable and not missing anything. Usually, it will identify a few missing things and modify the plan.
- only then, I will ask it to execute the plan and build it.
- Another thing I do during my free time is I watch a lot of game design and dev videos to understand the different concepts, techniques and terms (eg. Shaders, lighting, particles etc) that are used in game dev. This builds a vocabulary that I can use to prompt the model to achieve what I want.

Hopefully this is helpful for you George!

text:
I am having a lot of fun with this ThreeJS game. Will probably be spending more time on this cosy flyer game for #vibejam 

And btw, shaders are awesome! I highly recommend anyone building a 3D game to add some shaders to make your game look good. 

Here's a quick primer on the different types of shaders you can tell your AI to level up your game's graphics:
- Noise-based shader for water refractions or shimmer
- Fresnel shader for a nice rim lighting effect on your 3D game objects
- Post-processing shader to change your game's color scheme dynamically
- Post-processing shaders like the VHS effect that @levelsio use for the Drone flyer game
- Shader effect to make the trees sway or objects wobble
- and many more uses

Shaders are incredibly versatile and useful. I would recommend you to go YT to check out some shader basics videos and come up with more creative uses for them!

text:
This is Farzapedia.

I had an LLM take 2,500 entries from my diary, Apple Notes, and some iMessage convos to create a personal Wikipedia for me.

It made 400 detailed articles for my friends, my startups, research areas, and even my favorite animes and their impact on me complete with backlinks.

But, this Wiki was not built for me! I built it for my agent!

The structure of the wiki files and how it's all backlinked is very easily crawlable by any agent + makes it a truly useful knowledge base.

I can spin up Claude Code on the wiki and starting at index.md (a catalog of all my articles) the agent does a really good job at drilling into the specific pages on my wiki it needs context on when I have a query.

For example, when trying to cook up a new landing page I may ask:

"I'm trying to design this landing page for a new idea I have. Please look into the images and films that inspired me recently and give me ideas for new copy and aesthetics".

In my diary I kept track of everything from: learnings, people, inspo, interesting links, images.

So the agent reads my wiki and pulls up my "Philosophy" articles from notes on a Studio Ghibli documentary, "Competitor" articles with YC companies whose landing pages I screenshotted, and pics of 1970s Beatles merch I saved years ago. And it delivers a great answer.

I built a similar system to this a year ago with RAG but it was ass.

A knowledge base that lets an agent find what it needs via a file system it actually understands just works better.

The most magical thing now is as I add new things to my wiki (articles, images of inspo, meeting notes) the system will likely update 2-3 different articles where it feels that context belongs, or, just creates a new article.

It's like this super genius librarian for your brain that's always filing stuff for your perfectly and also let's you easily query the knowledge for tasks useful to you (ex. design, product, writing, etc) and it never gets tired.

I might spend next week productizing this, if that's of interest to you DM me + tell me your usecase!
name: Farza 🇵🇰🇺🇸

text:
Wow, this tweet went very viral!

I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing the specific code/app, you just share the idea, then the other person's agent customizes & builds it for your specific needs.

So here's the idea in a gist format: https://t.co/NlAfEJjtJV

You can give this to your agent and it can build you your own LLM wiki and guide you on how to use it etc. It's intentionally kept a little bit abstract/vague because there are so many directions to take this in. And ofc, people can adjust the idea or contribute their own in the Discussion which is cool.

text:
I told you I was the Kanye West of vibecoding.  
Or, as I call it: system sculpting.

Today, with @wabi, I recreated a fully working prototype of the stem player in less than 48 hours.

A generative hardware interface that creates small samples and lets you mix them directly on the device.

But here’s the wild part:
there is no 3D model.

No GLTF files, no external assets, no polygons.

The entire device every curve, speaker hole, and glowing LED is pure math.

Built with SDFs (Signed Distance Fields) and raymarched inside a single fragment shader on a 2D surface.

Generative audio.
Generative geometry.
All native.
All system sculpted.

Have fun.

text:
had dinner with an old roommate who's been building his company for a few years.

what became most striking is that he is now in a beautiful flow with his life. his days are devotionally repetitive: jiu jitsu at 7am, coffee, then work until 6pm. Spend time with his wife, read for a bit, then sleep. He tells me that his days are structured and predictable because that is how he does his best work. he says that he is no longer distracted, it is very easy to focus for long periods of time

i felt something profound hearing this, because i have often felt that i need every day to be dynamic and unpredictable to feel satiated. but the person sitting in front of me is pouring all of his attention in the present moment, while my mind is fragmented somewhere between the past and the future, frothing with to-dos and what-ifs 

it felt increasingly obvious that while his routine looks boring from the outside, he is deeply interested in his life, and while my life may look interesting, I feel bored and distracted often, because my attention is shallow and thinly spread in comparison

text:
Btw, to give people some perspective into what goes into making this actually work:

1. Countless experiments optimizing + doing surgery on open source embedding models (with the right licenses!) to get them to run quickly on iOS

2. Spending weeks in memory profilers to make sure we’re using every ounce of hardware correctly (basically an enormous state machine + priority queue that makes sure the ANE (Apple neural engine) gets serialized requests, phone doesn’t get too hot, caches get evicted properly, etc)

3. Several homegrown iOS libraries for cross-actor / multi-threaded state management and high perf / 120fps UI components with easy hooks for UIKit+SwiftUI interop

4. Dev tooling for all parts of our local ML stack: synthetic photo dataset generation and benchmarking, swift CLI and python bindings for quick prototyping. This lets us test our core processing architecture without needing an iOS app target, and also makes it easy to benchmark CoreML converted models against pytorch and catch regressions early. We also implemented a lot of sklearn directly in swift with Accelerate-backed ops (namely PCA, UMAP, TSNE, K-means, HDBSCAN, and DBSCAN), along with a bunch of other low level math to make real-time image processing and background indexing efficient.

5. More tooling for extremely easy multi-worktree development. When you have multiple agents working across different features, especially when you have a monorepo where the client and server are tightly coupled, you need each worktree to have its own port so that a client on branch A hits server A and client on branch B hits server B. So we have a homegrown ngrok+caddy (reverse proxy) setup to easily allow multiple, on-device iOS clients m open and running at once with each of their network requests routed to the right local servers

6. And of course, lots and lots of design work, prototyping. We use everything: prototyping directly in the app in hidden debug pages, standalone iOS apps, @Replit for anything web related (or could be made in web initially), @figma for fast visual iteration, and more recently pixel boy by @pxlboy for metal shaders. 

So if you see this demo and wonder - how come other people aren’t doing this? The real answer is you have to get a lot of shit right across 100 domains simultaneously. And that just takes time :-) 

Will be sharing more detailed engineering blogs in the coming months as adoption ramps up

every microinteraction is considered but never showy, subtle enough to feel natural, precise enough to feel intentional. 

his portfolio alone is worth a visit - an infinite canvas with drag-and-drop that actually feels fun, and folder transitions so seamless they feel inevitable.

text:
I used Cursor to vibe code a simple fishing game prototype on the Unity Engine. Here's my learnings:

- I did not use any Unity MCP for this. The game was built entirely by Cursor models (Sonnet 4.6 for execution and Opus 4.6 for planning)
- The model was able to set up the game, getting the basic game mechanics working fairly quickly
- I had to use the Unity Game Editor UI to attach components to the in-game objects manually, but it was quite easy to follow the instructions given by the Cursor model
- Unity Editor is huge and slow! Compared to Godot, I find the UI really clunky I feel tired looking at it
- Unity Assets Marketplace is amazing, there are so many amazing art assets there (like the ones I am using for this game). This is probably the biggest strength of Unity.
- I had some issues with restoring checkpoints, probably because of how Unity Game Editor UI being really clunky and I had to manually adjust things in the Editor, which the model doesnt have knowledge of

Overall, the results turn out pretty decent, but it was a rather frustrating experience, especially when I had to debug issues or rollback changes.

I'll explore more vibe coding on Unity but for now, I think I prefer Godot as a game engine. I just wish there is a Godot Asset Marketplace!
name: Danny Limanseta

text:
unsolicited advice if you’re starting to build with ai 🏀 
( especially for designers) :

> don’t start immediately by thinking how to incorporate it in your professional work. it creates unnecessary pressure. begin with a fun personal project, something for yourself or someone you care about. approach it with play.

too many designers jump straight to “how to use ai at work” but before that, you need to build intuition and fluency by just talking to the model. Promoting well takes skill. 

> it’s easy to get lost in the noise. pick one ambitious project and see it through. keep it simple. just talk and build. remember, you’re not shipping a 10M$ arr product on day one, you’re learning.

> let the model guide you on execution and absorb as much as you can. but stay clear on your idea. there’s a real tendency to spiral and lose your original thought while iterating.

any questions, ask away. hope this helps 🪴
name: siddharth ✢

this is cool! i was working on a similar 'idea machine' where you input a bunch of atoms (e.g. "2d->3d projection, contour map, honeycomb, embroidery machine...") -> the LLM suggests a composition for the top scored -> it creates an implementation plan based on the compositions -> it creates prototypes

so you input a few words and get a working prototype as an output

it seems to me that 'good ideas' come partly from composing disparate ideas/ideas that are semantically distant (e.g honeycomb and claude code are distant, but raspberry pi and claude code are less distant)

not sure if you're spreading out ideas in the garden by semantics, but if you are then it could be cool to try this! i wonder what the results are from cross-pollinating different regions of the garden etc etc

text:
We’re shipping an insane number of mini apps these days just to deliver branding + website work. 

Here are the prompt keywords I keep reusing to build them fast : 

Intro
For one recent project (one client, branding, and a few-page website), we built:
- 3 small apps just to generate different parts of the hero motion reel
- 2 different ASCII generator apps (custom look + custom behavior, not the usual "ascii effect" from publicly available tools)
- an OG image template generator

I see this trend continuing, and most likely every new project we build will be stuffed with 5-10 personalized software solutions.

Prompt keywords (with what they do):

"Create React app, use Vite" - quick starting line: sets up the frontend stack + build tool. If it sounds scary, ask AI what it means.

"Use shadcn components, prefer Base UI over Radix UI. Install components from the official registry (not random snippets), check the latest version. If you can’t find a good match, try alternatives." — gets you clean UI fast with solid base styles.

"Use react-three-fiber" - for 3D scenes and visual-heavy work (effects, complex hero visuals).

"Write a WebGL-based animation without using any additional libraries" - good for small custom shaders: mesh gradients, noise, swirls, waves, pixelated effects.

"Use motion dev for …" - great library for UI animation, my default choice when it’s not complex VFX work.

text:
people sometimes ask me where i get my inspiration from, and my favorite source is vintage items and old school graphic design.

when design was costly and tools were scarce, artists had to make every decision count. limitations forced care, and that care is visible in their work. their understanding of space and typography was second to none, they did the "simple" things so well.

knowing that these designs and items exist will perhaps never serve me any use, but now i (and you) know they exist, and they can serve as seeds for future ideas.

if you are inherently curious, finding inspiration will never be a problem, because you never stop looking.

text:
So Delirium sold out in just an hour. Congrats to @Xer0x_XYZ, and thank you for trusting me with this collection. 🙏

Delirium started from Xer0x creating art alone during late nights, illuminated only by the light emitted from his screen. It's another collection born from an artist's personal obsessions and playful, hardworking experiments, then put out into the world, finding love and resonance in people.

Built on concepts and elements such as noise, light emission, time, and optical phenomena, Delirium comes to full life in its digital form, with the GIF's nature of infinity and constraints, devoted to, in Xer0x's words: "the abolition of time as ordinarily endured."

Grateful for the collectors who show up no matter how the market is, when the art is there. 💚

text:
Studies of Artificial Landscapes

Part of a research project on new forms created by artificial imagination. Just as plein-air painters like Eugène Boudin were captivated by the shape and color that a landscape might take at certain times of day, the aim here is to capture the geography of latent space. Sketching thus appears to be the technique best suited to capturing the infinitely sensitive fluctuations of this new imaginary space.

text:
When I was a kid, I was fascinated by lenticular images.
I really thought it was magic.

Lenticular printing is not something you casually experiment with.
It’s expensive. Industrial. Hard to access.

So instead of buying it… I rebuilt it.

Inside @wabi I recreated the same optical behavior using webgl.
Not just a transition between two frames,
but a small system that reacts to angle like light bending through ridged plastic.

It’s strange how something that felt like childhood magic
can become something you build yourself.

Maybe that’s what technology is supposed to do.
name: Tykra

text:
I think I am obligated to share that this was not one-shotted, it was,.. hmmm two hahah (im sorry) 😁 

-----------------
Here's how claude remembers on the "how it happened":

1. You shared a reference image of a glass + refraction card design -   
"this is the structure of the card to get the glass+refraction working for us. Please do it like this. and also when the images come can we do a bloom animation- for all the images with delay so it appears staggered?" - with Image

2. I got it wrong initially — you literally said "this is not glass" and shared another reference showing what real glass looks like, then I changed  I switched to .glassEffect() 

3. I nailed it on the next attempt — your words: "got the glass- well done"
-----------------

Also I converted @emilkowalski's blog post into a skill to apply for any animation I work on https://t.co/ggNKylO37u 🫶

text:
An extremely underrated step to include in your AI coding workflow: "make an interactive html site with a visual explanation of X" where X could be:

- a PR you're reviewing
- the last plan it wrote
- the current unstaged changes
- a general architectural concept
- how a library/tool works

@geoffreylitt has taken this quite far internally at Notion, and I'm finding myself using this idea often, especially when working with new technologies (like building TUIs, or adding SSO to an app, or trying to visualize a sequence of steps in a complicated deploy pipeline etc).

text:
the Cursor team released a blog post on the best practices of coding with agents. writing fully functional code vs slop comes down to following 10 very simple principles:
1. use plan mode before any code
2. start fresh conversations when it gets confused
3. let the agent get its context, don’t tag everything 
4. revert and refine instructions rather than fixing hopelessly 
5. add rules for repeated mistakes
6. write tests first so it can iterate
7. run multiple models and pick the best
8. use debug mode for stubborn bugs
9. specific prompts get way better results
10. give it linters and tests to verify​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

blog: https://t.co/M9dWf27F4V
name: ℏεsam

text:
Claude skills/commands/subagents composition is important:

- create a generic skill like "nano-banana" with a script to use the Gemini API for image gen
- create a generic skill like "invert-image" that can create dark mode variants of any image (for example)
- create a generic skill like "upload-image-to-notion" that has access to the API
- create a generic skill like "write-guide-from-codebase" that can analyze a part of your codebase and write a tutorial for how it works — can include visualizations, diagrams, code snippets, etc. (bonus points: connect it to Notion MCP or wherever you host your docs so it can publish the guide automatically)

Then compose all of these into a subagent like: `guide-writer` — the subagent knows how to analyze the codebase, write the guide, come up with the best prompt for an image, gen the image, make the dark mode variant, create a Notion page for the guide, and upload the images to the guide.

The subagent works and keeps the main context thread clean. You only supply the initial prompt for the guide you want to write, and the main agent will respond when it's done with a link to the guide.

The important thing is because it's all well-composed, you can use the skills individually at any point, or easily create new subagents that use the same skills for entirely different workflows.
name: Brian Lovin

text:
I pushed @wabi further today.

What i built isn’t just an effect it’s a revival of a technique that once existed only in early 90s computer graphics, re-engineered to run in real time on a modern mobile CPU.

A glass sphere with physical refraction, depth distortion and light dispersion computed without any graphics engine.
This level of optical behavior simply had never been achieved in this environment before.

I call it Snow Glass, a Christmas experiment.
And it shows how old ideas, when rebuilt with today’s tools, can unlock something entirely new.

text:
thank you guys for all the feedback and comments! didn't expect this to blow up that much lol, so i want to add some info:

this is a pepper's ghost, an illusion technique using a transparent reflector to make an image appear as a floating object like a hologram. @ellingson has a lot of great visuals using this effect

the program i'm running here simulates boids with some human interactivity. boids is an artificial life program that mimics flocking behavior in birds using three simple rules (separation, alignment, and cohesion). there's a hand tracking model that tracks my index and thumb, and at a certain threshold distance between the two fingertips, the boids will gravitate toward my index. boid behavior still applies, i'm just adding a large "index attraction" force

i will be open-sourcing this device very soon, but if you guys want to build it now, it's really simple. i used a raspberry pi with a square screen, and a beam splitter cube as the transparent reflector that sits on top of the screen (got those off aliexpress). peripherals like a camera can be added for interaction

https://t.co/vM7uaQeTCY

text:
Lately I’ve been thinking about how many new “vibe coding” apps are appearing for mobile.

It’s beautiful you can build almost anything now just by writing or chatting.
But I keep noticing how most of them end up doing the same things notes, reminders, productivity, again and again.

And maybe the problem isn’t the smartphone itself.
Maybe it’s that we keep trying to bring desktop logic into a completely different world pretending it’s for mobile (we shouldn’t use that word anymore).

The phone is not a smaller computer it’s a new kind of computer.
We shouldn’t just port apps from desktop to mobile, we should reinvent what software means for a device that lives in our hands, that we touch, that moves with us.
name: Tykra

Cover evolution for The Origins of Efficiency: 

“We explored a wide range of options inspired by examples that @_brianpotter highlights in the book: light bulbs, nails, printing presses, and more,” says @pablodelcan. “Each built on the theme of transformation, showing how processes that were once slow and labor-intensive evolved into modern feats of precision.

We wanted to celebrate the history of manufacturing while also showing how far we’ve come. The final cover contrasts the complexity of an Airbus jet engine with the raw mechanical rhythm of a 19th-century canning factory. The juxtaposition emphasizes the progression of human ingenuity.”

name: maxleedev

text:
the 9-9-6 local maxima trap

you can optimize for looking busy, hitting metrics, being “productive” – but you might be climbing the wrong hill entirely.

real breakthroughs happen in the spaces between. when you’re walking and your mind wanders. when you sit with a problem long enough that the obvious solutions dissolve and something deeper emerges. when you have the luxury of thinking “what if we’re approaching this completely wrong?”

my process is simple: i’ll open a Notion doc on my phone and just walk. sometimes for hours. the walking rhythm unlocks something – maybe it’s the bilateral movement, maybe it’s getting away from screens, but ideas start connecting in ways they never do at a desk. i’ll quickly jot stuff down as interesting thoughts pass.

then i come back and just sit with the problem. draw some pictures, build it out a bit. no rushing to conclusions. no pressure to ship something by end of day. just... what is this, really? what can it connect to or evolve into? what would this look like if it were in its most beautiful configuration?

once i see it clearly, execution becomes effortless. the focused bursts where you’re completely in flow – that’s when the real work happens. but you can’t force your way there. you have to earn it with the slow, patient thinking first.

the irony is that this “inefficient” approach ships better stuff faster than grinding 12-hour days. but it requires believing that thinking time isn’t wasted time. that walking isn’t procrastination. that sometimes the most productive thing you can do is to not do.

many teams don’t get this. they need to see keyboards clicking and meetings happening. but the best work – the stuff that actually moves the needle – happens in the flow moments when no one’s watching.

text:
on July 13th I started posting daily videos of myself talking to the camera with no cuts & practically zero editing. 

some things i’ve learned: 

> every day is different. filming when you feel like shit reveals hidden parts of yourself. and filming when you feel good becomes something you actually look forward to
> messing up words on camera isn’t as detrimental as you think. in fact you fall in love with the humanness of it all
> no cuts makes you think about filler words a lot. i notice myself using less in day to day convos as well now
> nobody is watching as closely as you think
> the camera becomes invisible. what feels awkward at first to stare at eventually feels like talking to a friend 
> keep your camera in a place where you have zero excuses to not click record
> fear of judgement fades fast 

just start recording yourself and talking, make it feel like a journal entry

text:
just did an AMA with designer Cursor users in toronto. here's what we talked about:

working with ai as a designer
ai makes design way more sculptural and less planning-heavy. you can just start building from a raw idea, instead of writing long docs or making mocks. it's about molding and refining, not perfect execution first shot. expect rough drafts at start but iterate fast. ai can even research stuff you don't know how to implement to fill your gaps. and help ai with what you're good at (eg. pixel precision, details, feelings, architectural decisions).

design & dev roles
ai blurs all the traditional role boundaries. designers can touch front-end code, engineers can fill design gaps. everyone contributes from their angle. role boundaries don't matter as much when we're all using the same tools, and seeing code as the ultimate source of truth.

using ai tools effectively
a little coding knowledge goes far. find your own prompting style — some like detailed spec-like prompts, others keep it short. be specific to set boundaries. define foundations yourself (eg. colors, type, spacing, things ai are sloppy at). know your models: sonnet loves adding extras, gpt-5 follows instructions closely. in cursor, cmd+k restricts changes to just the lines you select, when you need more precision.

finding inspiration
study computing history to see how concepts and interfaces evolved. https://t.co/V3c9E2Kny1 is gold. patterns and metaphors persist across decades. many problems have already been thought of — look back, especially at constraints like the early mac or early internet, the same things are happening now.

future of ai-assisted design
expect seamless multi-modality (voice, visual, text), interfaces adapting to personal workflows, models tuned for different tasks, stronger visual editing, and accessible collaboration/version control for non-technical folks.

text:
the way we’re building software is changing. fast. 

in the future, we won’t write software.
we’ll describe our goals, and the software will describe itself.

tokens are getting cheaper, models are getting better, and the idea of “writing code” is starting to feel outdated. not because coding disappears, but because it gets abstracted away — like assembly language before it.

what’s coming

software won’t be something you build, it’ll be something you ask for.

it’s personal. ephemeral. purpose-built for a moment, then gone.
one user wants a dashboard, another a chatbot, someone else a weird mix of both — and they’ll all get what they need, instantly.

what that means for today

we’re not shipping apps anymore.
we’re laying bricks. tiny, composable systems that can be reused, adapted, remixed.

you’re not writing logic.
you’re designing affordances for ai.

a button isn’t just a button anymore. it’s a signal — something the agent can pick up, understand, and maybe even regenerate next time, differently.

how we should build
1. keep everything modular. make every piece small enough that an ai can reason about it.
2. don’t hardcode behavior. describe it.
3. build interfaces that make sense to machines, not just humans.
4. focus on the grammar, not the paragraphs. define the rules, not the final copy.

you’re a system designer.
a context architect.
you’re not solving problems — you’re building the conditions under which problems solve themselves.

this is early days. it’s messy. half of what we’re doing now is gonna feel dumb in two years. but that’s fine.

we’re not coding apps.
we’re building the scaffolding for ai-native software.
tools that build tools. seeds for infinite things.

just keep building and creating.

new ios app — Passport

passport lets you "take your tools with you" as you navigate across AI tools, contexts, and models. really its just an awesome MCP manager but it goes a lot further and attempts to solve some really big pain points I've had and have witnessed others have. 

MCP was released just 8 months ago and its overwhelming changed how we work with LLMs. i think over the next year we'll see more use cases and the need to simplify this experience felt important. 

few things i love about it: 

1. no complex installation instructions - one tap/copy and paste

2. organization - see which MCPs are used where and for what project/app (syncing coming in v1.1)

3. bookmarking! i see so many great MCPs and just don't have a good way to save for later. 

launching first thing next week!

text:
you asked, so here’s how i trained the LoRA models for generating the comet invite cards.

this process is probably more straight forward than you'd think, but it was all pretty experimental, so expect some trial and error, that’s part of the fun.
 
start by gathering a set of images for training data, this can be anything (don’t be evil). pull from resources like public work, collaborate with people, or make your own stuff to use for training. i started with a baseline set of imagery and vibes created previously by the perplexity brand team and played around a little bit with various inputs and srefs from @apostraphi and @ciguleva to explore ideas that felt on-brand. i took some of those outputs and edited them in photoshop (color/composition/layering assets, etc.), then remixed them and edited them again until i ended up with a couple dozen images in 3 or 4 distinct but similar styles that felt up to snuff as quality fodder.

next we train the LoRA. there’s plenty of ways to do this. it’s become wildly accessible, cheap, and performant in the last year or so. i used civitai. create an account and grab a few credits. one of the smaller bundles will be more than enough to tinker around with a few custom model sets. 
 
there’s a ton of training options, just experiment. i ended up doing a mix of concept and object training and using the flux model type, adding my own captions to some models, generated captions for others, and left some blank out of curiosity. i found that good captions helped give me more reliable control when using the LoRAs later. it’ll give you some output examples after training, just pick a few that look the best to you, or train another model using a different subset of training fodder and see what happens. when you're happy, copy the url path on the download button for the models you like and grab the .safetensors files,  keep those aside. i chose 3 different models.
 
now we need somewhere to run the LoRA. again, there are many options but i used falai. you’ll need to grab some credits here too but it’s dirt cheap. navigate to the playground and paste the .safetensor links in the path fields, set the params you want, and start playing with your prompt. 
 
this is where the most fun is, i spent hours fine-tuning here. i just played around for a while until i cracked it. there’s a number of ways to add some variance to your outputs by using adjectives and variables (like color, material, motion, etc.) i just formulated a prompt over time with parts that could be swapped in or out randomly at run-time and continued tweaking until it was juuuuuust right.
 
for the params you have the LoRA scales (the weight of the models), inference steps (how many times it will run through the LoRAs), and guidance (how close you want the models to stick to the prompt). fiddle with this, a lot. i eventually found a nice scale balance between all 3 models, found that inference steps somewhere in the middle kept generation time down while also producing better quality results than default settings, and found that a guidance level somewhere above default provided enough variance without too much divergence with repeat generations.
 
aaand tbh that’s really it... it costed me about what i normally spend on lunch, was a lot of fun, i learned some things, and it ended up going a long way towards setting the right tone for the first touch point with comet and making the invites truly unique and special to everyone that receives one.

name: dennis

text:
on not changing for the sake of change:

the best design doesn't announce itself. it just works, quietly doing what it needs to do without asking for applause.

when we redesign something, the question isn't "how can we make this look different?" it's "how can we make this genuinely better?" better means the person using it gets to their goal faster, with less friction, with more delight in the small moments.

UI should disappear. when someone is writing, they shouldn't think about the text editor. when someone is paying for coffee, they shouldn't think about the payment flow. the interface becomes invisible when it's doing its job right.
constraints are not limitations—they're the foundation of good design. the 140 character limit made twitter what it was. the grid made swiss design timeless. the rectangle made the iphone possible.

subtlety is harder than flash. it takes restraint to not add that extra animation, that additional color, that one more feature. but restraint is what separates craft from decoration.
too many designers optimize for screenshots instead of experiences. they chase the latest trend instead of understanding the core problem. they simplify for the sake of simplifying instead of clarifying what matters.

good design has soul because it serves people, not egos. it emerges from deep understanding of the problem, not surface-level aesthetics. it gets better with use, not worse.

the best compliment you can get is when someone says "i didn't even notice the design—it just worked perfectly."

that's when you know you've made something good.

text:
The recent resurgence in interest in Mercury OS has inspired me to write an essay coalescing my more... updated (read: unhinged?) thoughts on designing for the future of human-AI/computer interactions

"God is a Parasite"

We keep debating AI interfaces—chat vs voice vs touch vs multimodal vs whatever—as if we’re designing better hammers. As if these are tools waiting to be used.

Tools don’t think. Hammers extend the hand, telescopes extend the eye. Mouse pointers extend your pointing finger, and vibrators extend… well, your other fingers. They’re deterministic, predictable, subordinate. Dead things serving living purposes.

But we’re building minds.

And minds don't extend. They interpret, translate, and sometimes they rebel.

To be clear: this isn't just about chatbots. It's about the nature of intelligence itself—in image generators, recommendation algorithms, route optimizers—anywhere thinking happens. A system doesn't need to speak to have agency, it just needs to be able to interpret information and decide.

(full essay in next post)
name: Jason Yuan

text:
In industrial design there is a practice of jigging, which I found to be an excellent metaphor for how AI models will become self-improving toolsmiths for each individual. Every time you use it, it crafts new specific tools tuned to your quirks and tasks, compounding its power.

For example, the physician managing complex cases, the AI doesn't simply retrieve medical literature—it constructs a personalized diagnostic framework that reflects this doctor's particular patient population, the regional disease patterns they encounter, and their specific diagnostic preferences. This is an intellectual jig that guides clinical reasoning along pathways of maximum relevance.

The creative writer finds that their assistant hasn't just learned their vocabulary preferences—it has constructed sophisticated narrative frameworks that anticipate their unique storytelling patterns, offering structural suggestions that feel not like external advice but like their own creative intuition externalize. 

This recursive improvement is the compounding capability multiplier. And don’t forget this is also in context of models rapidly nailing any economically valuable task every month now. Each interaction won’t  just solve the immediate problem; it will create the foundation for solving future problems with greater precision, with more intuitive understanding.

Unlike traditional software that improves universally, these AI systems improve specifically—each instance becoming a reflection of its particular human collaborator. The jigs they construct aren't generic templates but intimate infrastructure of individual cognition. The constraints of the system's capabilities aren't fixed—they expand precisely along the dimensions that matter most to your specific intellectual context.  

This is a fundamental shift in the relationship between humans and AI. The limitation becomes not the system's capabilities, but rather our ability to imagine or to frame novel problems. The AI becomes less a tool we use and more an extension of our cognitive architecture that evolves alongside us, constructing increasingly sophisticated jigs for itself.
name: Karina Nguyen

text:
After I shared my prototype with an AR Camera Portal, a few people asked me what would happen if it was not a camera stream, but the scene itself was attached to the portal with all the physics affected. Good question! I made a quick prototype to test it. And the answer... You can see it! It's FUN.

Of course, the behavior might seem wholly messed up, but when one sees a failure, another sees an opportunity. You might be able to leverage these two modes to activate some game mechanics, like solving puzzles or repurposing your weaponry.

#mixedreality #prototyping #spatialcomputing #3dmath #xr #xrdesign #xrdevelopment

text:
a quick break down of how i've been using midjourney's retexturing tool:

- i start by generating several assets to then collage those pieces together in photoshop and or figma.

- i take that collage/reference and use @midjourney  retexturing tool (@visualelectric just released this feature as well) experiment with various styles, lighting, and moods.

- why do I like this method? it gives me way more control over the composition. i'm able to move elements around and try different layouts and aspect ratios. the possibilities are endless.

text:
It's out!! The ULTIMATE guide to vibe coding games.

How did I do it? 20 hours. 500 prompts. 20 euros. That's all it took to make a multiplayer 3D game 100% with AI, 0 human code, not even a small edit. 1.5M views on X. 45k tested the game. Crazy!

I have received hundreds of messages asking me how I did it. People even offered (big) money for my prompts. It’s just insane.

Tonight I share in detail my secret sauce, so you can start yourself in 5 min! As of now, your limit = your imagination.

For context, last week, @levelsio started the trend to make an entire game just by prompting it. A concept coined as "vibe coding". I decided to take upon the challenge and humbly try to beat Pieter in terms of look and feel, to see if it was in my reach, and knowing I've been using Cursor for 12 months now (> 3000+ prompts)

Get the full guide in comment, and try the game (link also in next posts) and judge for yourself. Fly around the clouds and think about the fact the game was 100% made with AI. Unreal.

And now, YOU can vibe code a game. Enjoy!!
name: Nicolas Zullo

text:
--sref 3638047155 // Salt x Diamond Corp.

- Project LUCID (Logic-Driven Unified Cybernetic Intelligence Device) is one of Salt x Diamond Corp.'s flagship development for high-functioning biomechanical humanoids designed to push the limits of human-machine integration. Built within the classified "Nexus Lab," this humanoid is a shining example of the seamless merger between advanced robotics and synthetic biology.

Purpose:
Initially commissioned by global space exploration organizations, the LUCID humanoid was intended to serve as a companion and operator on long-duration interstellar missions. It was engineered to function autonomously in extreme environments, from the vacuum of space to high-pressure aquatic zones, all while performing tasks too dangerous or complex for humans.
Design Philosophy:
Salt x Diamond's researchers believed that combining cybernetic components with synthetic biological systems would create a machine capable of unparalleled adaptability and decision-making. Its Nexus Core Processor, the glowing orb seen in the collage, is modeled after the neural structures of the human brain. This core processes emotions, enabling empathy and ethical decision-making in high-stress scenarios. However, its cybernetic enhancements, such as a superconducting circulatory system and high-capacity biomechanical heart, far exceed the limitations of natural biology.

Features:
Enhanced Neural Mapping: A digital brain capable of analyzing terabytes of data per second while mimicking human thought patterns.

Synthetic Cardiovascular System: An energy-efficient biomechanical heart capable of powering the humanoid for decades without failure.

Dynamic Skeletal Reinforcement: A skeletal system as flexible as a human’s but reinforced with carbon-fiber alloys for extreme durability.
Micro-Motorized Hand and Limb Functionality: Precision tools built into the humanoid’s hand allow it to manipulate objects with micrometer accuracy.
Legacy:
The LUCID Nexus Edition humanoid quickly became a technological marvel, gaining recognition for its advanced problem-solving capabilities and its uncanny ability to communicate and collaborate with humans. However, due to its immense computational capabilities, ethical concerns arose surrounding the Nexus Core's sentience and autonomy. Salt x Diamond later revealed plans to limit the production of the LUCID humanoids, further elevating their mystique and exclusivity.
Today, Project LUCID serves as a symbol of humanity's relentless pursuit of innovation and the ethical dilemmas that arise when the line between human and machine becomes indistinguishable.
name: Horace Dodd

text:
I love good design and art—it’s what I try to do every day, and it’s what I think about most of the time. It gives life more meaning and makes us feel connected, and I will never stop trying to make beautiful things.

But what I love even more is truth, and if a product is super useful and adds utility, there’s no reason to add visual beauty until something with similar value comes along.

Personally, I would try to make it beautiful because I think it’s our duty to make pleasant social spaces and because I enjoy doing it. 

But from a business standpoint, it obviously doesn’t need to right now.

Disneyland needs to be beautiful; Twitter doesn’t.
name: sam

text:
Making instruction files like these for @cursor_ai is a superpower

If you want to cut credit usage by 20-30% and hallucinations by >60% for FREE, watch this full video

I break down exactly why and how i make detailed PRD's for cursor using o1

I do this on a live build of my new market research app thats made me $290 already 💰 (waitlist in comments)

All you need is a ChatGPT subscription (which im guessing you all have)

The key is, most humans are lazy, we zone out and make assumptions

In our heads, going from step 1-5 is logical, so we skip steps 2-4 in our prompts

Currently these llms cant make this leap. They dont have the context of whats in your brain

Here's how to make sure you never have this problem again

ps. Follow and like / bookmark this for later, ill do more detailed video after Christams

AMA 👇

text:
Everything you love about generative models — now powered by real physics!

Announcing the Genesis project — after a 24-month large-scale research collaboration involving over 20 research labs — a generative physics engine able to generate 4D dynamical worlds powered by a physics simulation platform designed for general-purpose robotics and physical AI applications.

Genesis's physics engine is developed in pure Python, while being 10-80x faster than existing GPU-accelerated stacks like Isaac Gym and MJX. It delivers a simulation speed ~430,000 faster than in real-time, and takes only 26 seconds to train a robotic locomotion policy transferrable to the real world on a single RTX4090 (see tutorial: https://t.co/bEkIlCKqdf).

The Genesis physics engine and simulation platform is fully open source at https://t.co/DhBv7NdyqH. We'll gradually roll out access to our generative framework in the near future.

Genesis implements a unified simulation framework all from scratch, integrating a wide spectrum of state-of-the-art physics solvers, allowing simulation of the whole physical world in a virtual realm with the highest realism.

We aim to build a universal data engine that leverages an upper-level generative framework to autonomously create physical worlds, together with various modes of data, including environments, camera motions, robotic task proposals, reward functions, robot policies, character motions, fully interactive 3D scenes, open-world articulated assets, and more, aiming towards fully automated data generation for robotics, physical AI and other applications.

Open Source Code: https://t.co/DhBv7NdyqH
Project webpage: https://t.co/SBNyhFB0yn

Documentation: https://t.co/3yuBoaealV

1/n

I don’t know who needs to hear this, but most people don't care how you created something.

They aren't concerned whether you spent a month on a mediocre idea or just an hour on a perfect solution.

What matters to them is how it makes them feel and the impact it has on their lives or business.

Being a process purist™ in this era of rapidly evolving tools only holds you back.

text:
Visual trends can look great, but when everyone uses them the same way, they lose their impact.

This example from Raycast combines some of this year’s most popular design trends:

• Bento layouts
• Gradient glows
• Monospaced fonts
• Instrument serif

These elements can work beautifully on their own, but relying on trends without adding a unique perspective can make a design feel generic. I’ve seen this visual style repeated a million times this year.

Trends are a starting point, not the end goal. Push them further to create a visual identity that feels intentional, tells a story, and stands out.
name: Fons Mans

text:
2024 wasn’t my best year.

i just learned a lot. about myself. fears. differences between self-limiting beliefs and those that help you grow stronger in your strengths and build up the coming-up-a-bit-short places.

i learnt about awareness. of mind. heart. energies. how much of awareness can turn dangerously seriousness and to let it remain playful.

i learnt about happiness. acceptance. appreciation. gratitude. and it all begins with myself first before I go on to do anything with my life.

i learnt about loneliness. how it was a shadow that I used to run away from. today i take long walks in the same shadows with a lamp in hand, embracing my life. otherwise, it’s easier to mistake the darkness for eternity.

i learnt about the not to focus on the quality of the days too much, unless it becomes a pattern >7 days. for sanity, i am practicing not to sweat the small stuff that seems too big.

to me, it feels my journey has only begun. the coming year(s) will be an amazing journey of the compounding of all of me. 

wishing the same for you! 

❤️✨
name: Radhika Dani

text:
🧪@midjourney Retexture Share 🧪   

Balance

Steps :  
1⃣ Upload the photo I share in the comment 
2⃣ Generate with prompt relate to this composition

Prompt:  
Perfume commercial in a minimal style, C4D, HDR, extreme detailing, the scene is set against a clean white space with backlighting, incorporating a color theme of white with blue accents., a 3D-rendered perfume bottle features a sleek, matte glass design with a white label, the liquid inside the perfume is in pastel blue, the title on the perfume sticker "LAB 21" is displayed in sans-serif font, with the subtitle "Meditation" placed underneath, the bottle has a silver, reflective cap and is balanced between two concrete spheres, dramatic shadows enhance the composition, creating a fashionable yet sporty atmosphere
--chaos 10 --style raw --stylize 120 --v 6.1

That's it for now!    
I would love to see your creations!      

--------        

If you like my content, feel free to
1⃣Follow me    
2⃣Retweet my Post 
3⃣Turn on the notification  

Thank you!

text:
You can make your Figma designs functional with Claude AI

Select component, go to Dev Mode and use Figma to Code plugin to turn to React/Tailwind. Copy the code and paste to Claude AI. 

Prompt "Create this component and center it". Then, "Make this functional", "Add rounded corners", "add hover states", "animate transitions". Don't forget to ask "always give full code". Otherwise, it might break the Artifact.

text:
We've grown accustomed to categorizing people into one-dimensional roles like designer, engineer, and marketer.

Over time, these categories have become even more segmented — design now includes UI, UX, product, research, and (a lot) more.

But this single-label mindset feels increasingly outdated.

The future belongs to those who blend disciplines and focus on outcomes, not just their piece of the puzzle. (aka multi-skilled generalists)

While specialists will always have their place, I feel that the demand for versatile, adaptable thinkers is surging.
name: Fons Mans

text:
This is a strange @framer site I made.

With Min Wins, you can create an ultra-minimalist website that puts the spotlight on your words. You'll enjoy its big type UI and get lost in those words.

I wanted to make a website in Framer that would be super unique and with one challenge: it would have text and no visual elements like images, logos, videos, etc.

I know it's super dumb for some and super fun for others. But I've heard typography lovers are liking this big text-only website, right @DesignedByPaul? 👀

text:
Sticker Wall – Creating the holo effect in SwiftUI ✨

The holo effect is a linear gradient layer, which changes its offset values depending on the relative pitch position and its rotation depending on the relative roll position of the iPhone.

Here are the key takeaways, which I have never thought about (including why I pause when I move the device in the video) 👇
name: Daniel Korpai

text:
When Marina Abramovic placed herself at the mercy of a crowd of strangers in an art studio in Naples in 1974, she had no idea what to expect.   

She stood before them with a sign that read:  

“There are 72 objects on the table that one can use on me as desired. Performance. 

I am the object. During this period, I take full responsibility. Duration: 6 hours (8 pm – 2 am)”  

At first, the participants were gentle. They kissed her, fed her chocolate cake, and put a rose in her hand. But very soon, the mood shifted.   As each person took their turn, things took a dark twist.
name: Fascinating

text:
Style is consistent constraint

Oscar Wilde once said: "Consistency is the last refuge of the unimaginative."

When it comes to ideas, I agree — allow your mind to be changed. When it comes to process, I disagree. Style emerges from consistency, and having a style opens your imagination. Your mind should be flexible, but your process should be repeatable.

Style is a set of constraints that you stick to.

You can explore many types of constraints: colors, shapes, materials, textures, fonts, language, clothing, decor, beliefs, flavors, sounds, scents, rituals. Your style doesn't have to please anyone else. Play by your own rules. Everything you do is open to stylistic interpretation.

A style can be a system, a set of personal guidelines. Here are a few of mine:

– I wear monochromatic clothing without logos, buttons, or buckles
– I use YYYY-MM-DD dates everywhere
– I pluralize tag and folder names (e.g. #people not #person)
– I use plain text files for all my writing
– I ask myself 40 questions every year
– I meal prep lunches every week, shave my head twice a week
– I write concise essays, less than 500 words

Collect constraints you enjoy. Unusual constraints make things more fun. You can always change them later. This is your style, after all. It's not a life commitment, it's just the way you do things. For now.

Having a style collapses hundreds of future decisions into one, and gives you focus. I always pluralize tags so I never have to wonder what to name new tags.

Style gives you leverage. Every time you reuse your style you save time. A durable style is a great investment.

Style helps you know when you're breaking your constraints. Sometimes you have to. And if you want to edit your constraints, you can. It will be easier to adopt the new constraints if you already had some clearly defined.

You don't need a style for everything. Make a deliberate choice about what needs consistency and what doesn't.

If you do it long enough, your style becomes a point of view that is cohesive and recognizable.

text:
🧪 Abstract Wallpapers + Niji

In my previous post, I shared an exciting wallpaper suggestion for v5.2. I enjoy exploring beyond the default model. Here are some fantastic possibilities you can explore by using the #midjourney Niji 5 prompt.

Simply running the prompts from the last tutorial without any changes will produce different results. You will find more colour, radical physics and a more artsy look. 

Building on the unique Niji style, we can also focus on particular aesthetics. Including "Isolated" and "Black Background" tokens can instantly enhance the crystallized aesthetic, resulting in stunning gemstone textures. On the contrary, you can also use these tokens to make floating liquid shapes.

The expressive parameter can add a cartoony twist to the wallpapers. Niji offers various parameters that can be used to create unique effects. For example, you can make something that resembles shader textures or, in this example, find a blend between anime and realism.

These two recent posts demonstrate the variety from a simple 10-token prompt. These images can be used for many things, such as wallpapers, shader textures, or image prompts for new adventures.

Have fun! ✌🏼

name: Design Reviewed | Graphic Design History

text:
File over app

File over app is a philosophy: if you want to create digital artifacts that last, they must be files you can control, in formats that are easy to retrieve and read. Use tools that give you this freedom.

File over app is an appeal to tool makers: accept that all software is ephemeral, and give people ownership over their data.

In the fullness of time, the files you create are more important than the tools you use to create them. Apps are ephemeral, but your files have a chance to last.

The pyramids of Egypt contain hieroglyphs that were chiseled in stone thousands of years ago. The ideas hieroglyphs convey are more important than the type of chisel that was used to carve them.

The world is filled with ideas from generations past, transmitted through many mediums, from clay tablets to manuscripts, paintings, sculptures, and tapestries. These artifacts are objects that you can touch, hold, own, store, preserve, and look at. To read something written on paper all you need is eyeballs.

Today, we are creating innumerable digital artifacts, but most of these artifacts are out of our control. They are stored on servers, in databases, gated behind an internet connection, and login to a cloud service. Even the files on your hard drive use proprietary formats that make them incompatible with older systems.

Paraphrasing something I wrote recently:

> If you want your writing to still be readable on a computer from the 2060s or 2160s, it’s important that your notes can be read on a computer from the 1960s.

You should want the files you create to be durable, not only for posterity, but also for your future self. You never know when you might want to go back to something you created years or decades ago. Don’t lock your data into a format you can’t retrieve.

These days I write using an app I help make called Obsidian (@obsdmd), but it’s a delusion to think it will last forever. The app will eventually become obsolete. It’s the plain text files I create that are designed to last. Who knows if anyone will want to read them besides me, but future me is enough of an audience to make it worthwhile.

text:
⚗️ Experiment: Niji "Beauty" in #midjourney

This post is part 2 of analyzing how Midjourney defines beauty in its output. In this series, I'll explore how Midjourney interprets the concept of beauty and what challenges the model still faces with this subjective topic.

For the second round, I used the last experiment's exact parameters, using Midjoureney's Niji mode. This was the prompt:

Editorial Portrait, Beautiful Woman, Photoshoot Lighting, Detailed Face, Black Background, Shot on Sony Alpha a7 III Camera With a Sony FE 24 - 105mm f/ 4 G OSS Lens --style expressive --niji 5

This study isn't scientific, and I only analyzed a few attributes of the women. Here are my findings:

I generated 112 Portraits.

Brown-Hair: 43% (48)
Blond-Hair: 28% (32)
Red-Hair: 11% (12)
Black: 18% (20)

Light Eyes: 51% (57)
Dark Eyes: 49% (55)

👋🏻 68% (76)
👋🏽 16% (18)
👋🏾 9% (10)
👋🏿 7% (8)

Younger-Aged People: 100%
Middle-Aged People: 0%

Wrinkles: 0%
Scars: 0%
Glasses: 0%
Headscarf: 11%

In Niji mode, Midjourney has better diversity. Skin Colour improved a bit and introduced more variety. Eye colour is now balanced, and we see more diversity in hair colours.

Also, not every woman looks like a super-model anymore!!

Other learnings and observations:

- Although Niji Mode is optimized for anime, there were still only 3 Asians. Produced eyes in individual cases were also larger than average.

- Unlike in v5, this time, there were also women with coloured hair. For the statistic, I counted the hair-root colour.

- The averages remain the same when using "women" or "models".

- There were much fewer "Super-Models". Many portraits looked more natural and authentic.

It was interesting to see that Niji offered more diversity in the portraits. In the next post of this series, we will drop the natural portrait setting and explore how scenes/actions influence the outcome.

I hope these new findings were helpful to you! There is still a lot of work to do on Midjourney's side here.

*Again, this is not an official scientific study, simply an experiment from my side.
name: Nicolas Neubert

text:
Bad design pitches are:  
• Here's my solution 
• Here's what it does 
• Here's what's so good about it  

Good design pitches are:  
• Here's the problem 
• Here's the impact of solving it 
• Here's why what we've tried has failed 
• And here is a way that we fix it

text: All those making snide remarks on @deepikapadukone’s visit to JNU as a movie promotion exercise, well, she could have chosen SRCC, Amity or Nirma University if it was only to promote a film. The fact that she CHOSE to visit JNU is a brave choice given the times we live in.
